curriculum_alignment.subject: mathematics
curriculum_alignment.subtopic: set notation, union, intersection, complement, subsets, Venn diagrams
curriculum_alignment.seeds_of_change: Use correct set notation and symbols (∈, ∉, ⊆, ⊂, ∪, ∩, complement) to describe membership and relationships, and represent unions/intersections/complements accurately using Venn diagrams to support clear reasoning about categories and fairness in real-world grouping decisions. :contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0}

verse_ality.driving_question: How can set logic help us group, compare, and make fair decisions about who belongs where?

knowing_world.focus: Sets are collections of distinct elements. Students learn membership and subset symbols, and use union, intersection, and complement to reason about groups, representing relationships clearly with Venn diagrams.
key_concepts: Set as a collection of distinct elements
Universal set (U) and complement: A′ (or A−)
Venn diagrams as visual representations of set relationships
activities: Identify subsets: decide whether {2,4} ⊂ {2,4,6} and whether {2,5} ⊂ {2,4,6}.

human_story.focus: Set logic supports real decisions about categories (clubs, voting groups, eco-certified products, eligibility rules). Students consider how set-based policy rules can support fairness and clarity in public decisions.
justice_prompts: A city council offers free public transport to residents earning < £20,000 (Set A) and students (Set B). Is someone in both groups counted twice or once, and how can set logic help ensure fairness in policy design?

expressive_self.focus: Students express set thinking by making their own real-life Venn diagrams, writing dialogues using set language, and creating metaphors for sets and elements.
activities: Draw a real-life Venn diagram (e.g., people who like tea / coffee / both) and label sets and elements using names.
Write a short dialogue using set language: A = club members, B = volunteers; discuss A ∩ B and A ∪ B.
Create a set metaphor (e.g., set = bookshelf; elements = books) and record a 20-second explanation with an added twist.

designed_world.focus: Students use an interactive tool to choose set properties and drag numbers 0–25 into the correct Venn regions, testing understanding through multiple configurations and peer challenges.
tools: Canva embed: Interactive Venn Diagram Tool (choose properties for sets A and B; drag numbers 0–25; use Check)
activities: Set A = Prime numbers; Set B = Multiples of 3. Place 2, 3, 6, 9 and justify each region choice.
Try Set A = Square numbers; Set B = Odd numbers. Describe what you notice.
Challenge a peer: swap your dropdown settings and see if they can solve your version correctly.
Use the Check button to test your understanding; note any surprises or misconceptions.
design_challenges: Create your own set-property configuration and write a short justification for at least 5 numbers placed in different regions.

regulation_supports: Wrist stretches and a neck tilt to each side.
Eye refresh: alternate focus between a close object and a distant object for 30 seconds.

pathways.name: Final Task - Set Logic Puzzle
pathways.focus: Design a set logic puzzle for others using two set properties and Venn diagram placement for numbers 0–25, plus symbolic statements to test membership and reasoning.
products: Two chosen set properties for A and B (e.g., Squares and Odds)
A completed Venn diagram placing each number 0–25 into the correct region
Three true/false set statements using symbols (e.g., 9 ∈ A ∩ B, 8 ∈ A′, 12 ∈ A ∪ B)
A short reflection describing one misconception caught using Check answers and how you fixed it
assessment_criteria: Conceptual Understanding — Explains region logic; interprets union/intersection/complement statements correctly
Fluency & Accuracy — Numbers placed correctly; consistent and correct use of ∈, ∪, ∩, ⊂/⊆ and complement notation
Application — Connects sets to real-world sorting/eligibility/grouping contexts
Independence & Reflection — Identifies and corrects logic errors; reflects on learning

required: Confidence with basic number properties (e.g., primes, multiples, squares, odd/even)
Comfort reading and writing lists of numbers
Willingness to check and revise placements when errors are found

builds_toward: Set notation in algebra and functions (domains and ranges)
Logical reasoning with categories and constraints
Data classification and fairness in eligibility rules
